title: Human Heart Nuclei Isolation for DroNc-seq
author: Stephanie Lozano
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.vgue3ww

steps.instruction: For each nuclei preparation, aliquot 20 mL of EZ-Prep Lysis Buffer and prepare 7 mL NSB. Place both on ice to chill while preparing tissue.
Weigh tissue to be prepared (Usable numbers of nuclei have been retrieved from 30-40 mg of heart tissue). Prior to douncing, mincing tissue with a scalpel or razor blade can be useful.
Place tissue in douncing mortar with 2 mL ice-cold EZ-Prep lysis buffer. Dounce 25 strokes with Pestle A.
Transfer from mortar to 15 mL conical tube. Wash mortar into tube with 2 mL additional ice-cold EZ-Prep. Incubate sample on ice 5 minutes.
Aspirate supernatant. Wash and resuspend pellet with 2 mL ice-cold EZ-Prep.
Transfer back to douncing mortar and dounce 25 strokes with Pestle B.
Wash and resuspend pellet with 4 mL ice-cold EZ-Prep, incubate on ice 5 minutes.

At this point, reserve a 10 µL sample to check for nuclei (see below: “Counting Nuclei”). a. If additional douncing is needed, repeat steps 7-9 until an appropriate suspension is achieved. The samples used here required 25-35 additional dounces with Pestle B. b. If membrane fragments are still present after additional douncing, the suspension may be filtered before transferring to NSB. The suspensions obtained here were sequentially filtered once with a 20 µm filter, then once with a 10 µm filter into fresh 50 mL conical tubes.
Aspirate supernatant, wash and resuspend pellet in 4mL ice-cold NSB.
Aspirate supernatant and resuspend pellet in 2 mL ice-cold NSB.
Filter nuclei into a fresh 50 mL conical tube using a 10 µm filter. Keep on ice until needed for experiment.

For a 10 µL hemocytometer, mix 10 µL nuclei with 10 µL DAPI and 10 µL WGA in a microcentrifuge tube. Load 10 µL into hemocytometer and count using fluorescent and phase contrast images overlaid, if possible. This should allow accurate assessment of the number of bare nuclei in suspension in comparison to the amount of membrane fragments or unstained debris.
After counting, dilute nuclei to desired concentration using remaining NSB. The tissue samples used here yielded 300-400K nuclei/mL of final suspension.
